name: ComposeCatalog
tagline: A single scrolling screen that tours every common Jetpack Compose + Material 3 component as a labelled exhibit.
whatItDoes: You see one vertically-scrolling screen, built inside a Scaffold (with a TopAppBar, a FloatingActionButton, and a Snackbar), that walks top-to-bottom through every everyday component: Text (typography, color, weight, italic, alignment, ellipsis), the five Button emphasis levels plus IconButton, three FloatingActionButton sizes, Checkbox / RadioButton group / Switch, continuous / stepped / range Sliders, TextField and OutlinedTextField (label, leading icon, password masking), Surface and three Card styles, the Column / Row / Box layout primitives, Icon vs Image, circular and linear progress indicators (indeterminate and determinate), AssistChip / FilterChip / SuggestionChip / Badge, an AlertDialog opened from a button, and a horizontally-scrolling LazyRow. The interactive controls actually work (tap the FAB for a Snackbar, flip the Switch, toggle the FilterChip, open the dialog). There is no navigation, network, or persistence — it is purely a component reference.
mentalModel: Compose ships a Material 3 component for almost every common UI need; learn the small set of parameters each one takes (value/onValueChange, a content slot, a few style knobs) and you can assemble most screens.

screens.name: CatalogScreen
screens.role: The whole catalog: a Scaffold (TopAppBar + FAB + Snackbar) wrapping a verticalScroll Column of component sections; owns the demo state via remember.

screens.name: ComponentSection
screens.role: A reusable frame that draws a title + one-line caption around each demo, supplied via a trailing @Composable content slot.

screens.name: MainActivity
screens.role: The single entry point: setContent { ComposeCatalogTheme { CatalogScreen() } }.

concepts.plain: 'ComponentSection(title, caption) { …demo… }' takes the demo UI as a trailing '@Composable () -> Unit' lambda — the exact same idea as 'Button { Text(...) }', 'Card { … }', and 'Scaffold { innerPadding -> … }'.

'Scaffold' provides the standard screen skeleton: 'topBar', 'floatingActionButton', and 'snackbarHost' slots plus an 'innerPadding' you must apply so content isn't hidden behind the bars or system insets.

Same tap, five looks: 'Button' (filled, highest), 'FilledTonalButton', 'ElevatedButton', 'OutlinedButton', and 'TextButton' (lowest). 'IconButton' wraps a tappable 'Icon'. 'enabled = false' greys any of them out.

'Checkbox', 'Switch', 'Slider', and the text fields are driven by a value parameter ('checked' / 'value') and report changes through a callback; here the value lives in 'CatalogScreen' via 'remember { mutableStateOf(...) }'.

Material 3 takes 'progress' as a LAMBDA: 'CircularProgressIndicator(progress = { 0.66f })' and 'LinearProgressIndicator(progress = { 0.4f })'. Omitting 'progress' gives the indeterminate (spinning / animated) variant.

'Icon' draws a small VECTOR glyph tinted by 'tint' (a 'material-icons-core' asset like 'Icons.Default.Favorite'); 'Image' draws a picture (a Painter) at its own colors. They are different composables for different jobs.

A Snackbar is shown by launching a coroutine ('scope.launch { snackbarHostState.showSnackbar(...) }') because 'showSnackbar' is suspend. An 'AlertDialog' is added to the composition only WHILE a boolean state ('showDialog') is true.

concepts.term: Composition: nesting & writing your own slot
concepts.plain: The whole 'CatalogScreen' is one nested TREE — a 'Scaffold' wrapping a scrolling 'Column' of ~13 'ComponentSection's, each nesting Rows/Columns and a demo component. Rather than repeat the title/caption/divider markup 13 times, it is EXTRACTED into one @Composable, 'ComponentSection', which takes a 'content: @Composable () -> Unit' SLOT and renders it between the title and divider — the same content-lambda mechanism 'Card { }', 'Button { }', and 'Scaffold { }' use, here written by hand.

Pick the container by need: 'Column' stacks a few items vertically and 'Row' lays a few side-by-side (both compose ALL children — add verticalScroll or use a Lazy list if there are many). 'LazyColumn' / 'LazyRow' are SCROLLING lists that only compose the visible rows — use them for long or data-driven content. 'Box' overlaps/centers children (each positions itself with Modifier.align). 'Scaffold' is the screen skeleton: topBar / bottomBar / FAB slots plus innerPadding — use it as a screen's root and put a Column or LazyColumn inside it.

files.role: The entire catalog: the reusable ComponentSection frame, the CatalogScreen (owns the demo state), MainActivity, and two @Preview functions.
ComposeCatalogTheme — the Material 3 wrapper supplying colorScheme + typography that every component reads from.
Compose BOM + Material 3, plus the extra 'material-icons-core' library that supplies the Icons.Default.* vector glyphs.
Plain-English list of every component group, key files, what to inspect, and how to run.

highlights.title: ComponentSection — the reusable exhibit frame (slot pattern)
highlights.explain: Each component group is shown inside this little frame. It draws a 'titleMedium' heading and a muted 'bodySmall' caption, then calls 'content()' — the demo UI the caller passed as a trailing '@Composable () -> Unit' lambda — and finishes with a 'HorizontalDivider'. This IS the content-slot pattern that 'Button', 'Card', and 'Scaffold' all use.

highlights.title: Scaffold: TopAppBar + FAB + Snackbar
highlights.explain: 'Scaffold' supplies the screen skeleton. 'topBar' holds a 'TopAppBar(title = { Text(...) })'; 'floatingActionButton' holds a 'FloatingActionButton' whose tap 'scope.launch'es 'snackbarHostState.showSnackbar(...)' (showSnackbar is suspend); 'snackbarHost' renders the 'SnackbarHost'. The trailing '{ innerPadding -> }' is the content slot, and that padding must be applied so content clears the bars.

highlights.title: Text — typography, color, weight, alignment, ellipsis
highlights.explain: 'style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineSmall' pulls a named role from the theme instead of hardcoding a size. You can also override individual properties: 'fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold', 'color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary', 'fontStyle = FontStyle.Italic', 'textDecoration = TextDecoration.Underline', 'textAlign = TextAlign.Center', and 'maxLines = 1' + 'overflow = TextOverflow.Ellipsis' to clip long text with a '…'.

highlights.title: Buttons — five emphasis levels + IconButton
highlights.explain: The same 'onClick' rendered five ways — 'Button' (filled), 'FilledTonalButton', 'ElevatedButton', 'OutlinedButton', 'TextButton' — plus a 'Button' with a leading 'Icon' in its content slot, a disabled 'Button(enabled = false)', and an 'IconButton'. Choose the emphasis that matches how important the action is.

highlights.title: Text fields — TextField & a password OutlinedTextField
highlights.explain: Both fields are 'hoisted': their 'value' is screen state and every keystroke goes up through 'onValueChange'. The filled 'TextField' shows a floating 'label', a 'leadingIcon', and 'singleLine'; the 'OutlinedTextField' adds a 'placeholder', a 'PasswordVisualTransformation()' that masks input as dots, and 'KeyboardOptions(keyboardType = KeyboardType.Password)'.

highlights.title: FilterChip (nullable icon slot) + BadgedBox
highlights.explain: 'FilterChip' toggles 'selected' state on click and only shows a leading checkmark WHEN selected — note 'leadingIcon = if (filterSelected) { { Icon(...) } } else null', a nullable composable-lambda slot. 'BadgedBox(badge = { Badge { Text("8") } })' anchors a small count Badge to the top-right corner of the bell Icon it wraps.

highlights.title: Dialog — state-driven AlertDialog
highlights.explain: A 'Button' sets 'showDialog = true'. The 'AlertDialog' is in the composition only while that boolean is true: 'onDismissRequest' (tap-outside / back) and both the confirm and dismiss 'TextButton's set it back to false. Driving a dialog from a boolean state is the canonical Compose idiom.

newVsPrev: ComposeModernUI taught HOW Compose works (composables, recomposition, state hoisting, previews) on a tiny three-component screen. ComposeCatalog widens that to the full vocabulary: it puts essentially every common Material 3 component on one screen — buttons, selection controls, sliders, text fields, cards/surfaces, icons/images, progress, chips/badges, dialogs, a Scaffold with TopAppBar/FAB/Snackbar, and a LazyRow — each in a reusable ComponentSection frame, so you can see what each widget looks like and learn its key parameters in one place. It is the 'which widget, and what knobs?' reference; ComposeModifiers is the companion 'how do I size and arrange them?' reference.
tryThis: Run the app, then tap the FloatingActionButton (bottom-right) and watch a Snackbar slide up — it is launched in a coroutine because 'showSnackbar' is a suspend function.
Flip the 'Switch' and toggle the 'Checkbox' / 'RadioButton' group: the labels update because each control's value is screen state read by the UI.
Drag the stepped Slider — it snaps to 0–5 because of 'valueRange = 0f..5f, steps = 4'. Change 'steps' to 9 and a 'valueRange' of '0f..10f' to get 0–10.
Tap the 'FilterChip' and watch a leading checkmark appear / disappear — that is the 'leadingIcon = if (selected) { { Icon(...) } } else null' nullable slot at work.
Open MainActivity.kt in Android Studio and view the tall 'Catalog' @Preview to scroll the whole catalog without an emulator.
Edit the same components live in Playground/playground.html — change a Button to an OutlinedButton, or a Text's style, and watch the preview update as you type.
gotchas: 'TopAppBar' is an experimental Material 3 API, so 'CatalogScreen' is annotated '@OptIn(ExperimentalMaterial3Api::class)'. Forgetting the opt-in is a compile error, not a crash.
Determinate progress uses a LAMBDA: 'CircularProgressIndicator(progress = { 0.66f })'. The old bare-Float overload is deprecated; passing a plain Float will not match the current signature.

The 'Icon' demos need vector assets that 'material3' no longer bundles, so this project adds 'material-icons-core' in libs.versions.toml / build.gradle.kts. Without it 'Icons.Default.*' won't resolve.
All state is plain 'remember' (not 'rememberSaveable'), so toggles reset on a configuration change. That's deliberate — this is a transient catalog, not data worth persisting.
This screen owns its control state inline for convenience. That is the opposite of the ComposeModernUI lesson; in a real app you'd hoist that state into a stateless child or a ViewModel.
runTest: Build and install from the project root with './gradlew installDebug' (or './gradlew assembleDebug' to just build the APK), then scroll the catalog and try the interactive bits: tap the FAB for a Snackbar, flip the Switch, toggle the FilterChip, and open the AlertDialog. Run the example unit test with './gradlew testDebugUnitTest'. Fastest of all: open MainActivity.kt in Android Studio and view the 'Catalog' @Preview in the design pane (no emulator needed). There are no deep links in this project.
